findings.message: The ADR selects LanceDB as the first candidate stack in D2, but the reassessment trigger describes hitting sqlite-vec-style full-scan limits as a reason for a future migration to `LanceDB / USearch`. That makes the rollout baseline ambiguous: readers cannot tell whether Phase 1 starts on LanceDB as decided or on sqlite-vec with LanceDB deferred.

T007 assigns the workspace scanner/extractor to `BuildIndexInteractor`, but the usecase design only gives interactors `EmbeddingPort` and `SemanticIndexPort` and `BuildIndexCommand` only carries `workspace_root`. With no fragment-extraction port or pre-extracted fragments in the command, implementation must either put filesystem scanning inside the usecase interactor, contradicting the stated pure-orchestrator/no-I/O hexagonal design, or leave `dup-index build` without a way to obtain fragments.

IN-01/IN-02 name the port implementations as `EmbeddingAdapter` and `SemanticIndexAdapter`, but the same design later declares the ports as `EmbeddingPort` and `SemanticIndexPort` in IN-07 and the usecase catalogue. This conflates adapter and port names at the spec layer, so the infrastructure tasks are grounded in the wrong named design elements.

T009 still says `dup-check` accepts diff fragments via `--file <path>` while the section narrative and the same sentence say the concrete interface is `--files-from <newline-separated-file>`. That leaves the required CLI surface contradictory for the implementation task.

T003 requires `BuildIndexInteractor` to walk the filesystem and extract `CodeFragment` values, but T007 is later defined as the task that implements the scanner/extractor that `BuildIndexInteractor` calls. With T007 depending on the T003 interface, T003 cannot be completed as written without doing T007's work first or duplicating it.

MeasureQualityInteractor is specified as a pure orchestrator that delegates entirely to EmbeddingPort and SemanticIndexPort, but the measure-quality command/API is driven by a workspace_root and even has an Io error variant. With no fragment-extraction port, pre-extracted fragments, or helper task for measure-quality, the implementation must either do filesystem scanning inside this supposedly pure interactor or cannot obtain the Rust source fragments needed for AC-03.

SimilarityScore is defined as a cosine similarity score constrained to [0.0, 1.0], and the plan tests rejection of negative values, but raw cosine similarity can be negative and AC-03 requires measuring the cosine similarity distribution. This makes valid cosine results unrepresentable unless the design explicitly normalizes them before constructing SimilarityScore.

The measure-quality design promises a false_positive_rate, but MeasureQualityCommand only supplies workspace_root and T009 only wires --workspace-root. A false-positive rate requires a definition of positives/negatives or labelled/evaluation input; with only an unlabeled workspace the task can output a score distribution, but not a meaningful false-positive rate for the ADR's PoC decision.

T007 places the recursive Rust-file scanner in `libs/usecase/src/semantic_dup.rs` and has the interactors perform filesystem traversal. That is a wrong layer placement for this repository's hexagonal convention: usecase is supposed to stay an orchestration layer and filesystem I/O belongs outside it or behind an infrastructure port. Implementing this plan as written will bake a layer violation into the implementation plan.

The plan says T007 depends only on T001, but the same task also surfaces failures as `BuildIndexError::Io` and replaces the `BuildIndexInteractor` stub. Those symbols are introduced by T002/T003, so T007 cannot be implemented or tested as a T001-only task. The dependency ordering is internally inconsistent and can send implementers into an impossible task split.

T007 requires the workspace scanner/extractor to live in `libs/usecase/src/semantic_dup.rs` and use `std::fs`, and states that this is accepted. That contradicts the required hexagonal convention's Usecase Layer Purity Rules, which explicitly forbid `std::fs::*` in usecase and direct file reading to CLI or an adapter boundary. Implementing the plan as written would bake in a usecase-purity/layer violation.

S2 says `MeasureQualityInteractor` is a pure orchestrator that delegates entirely to `EmbeddingPort` and `SemanticIndexPort`, but T003 later says it walks `MeasureQualityCommand.workspace_root` to extract fragments. With no extraction port or pre-extracted fragments in the command, the plan is internally contradictory about whether this interactor performs filesystem scanning or remains a pure orchestrator.

`BuildIndexInteractor` is still documented as scanning workspace Rust files, but the plan explicitly says all interactors are pure usecase orchestrators with no filesystem I/O and that extraction is done by the infrastructure helper before constructing `BuildIndexCommand`. If implementers follow this catalogue text, the usecase layer will own filesystem scanning, contradicting the hexagonal split fixed elsewhere in the plan.

T001 says `SemanticDupError` should be implemented with `Debug/Clone/Copy/Display/Error` “as specified in domain-types.json”, but the domain catalogue only declares `Debug`, `Display`, and `Error` for `SemanticDupError`. Following the task text would add trait implementations that the catalogue does not specify, so the implementation plan and the type contract disagree.

AC-01 requires `sotp find-similar` to let callers specify `k`, but the CLI task only lists parsing fragment text / `--file` for find-similar and `--workspace-root` for build. Since T008 is the CLI wiring task that claims to cover AC-01, the implementation plan does not actually instruct implementers to expose the required top-k option.

AC-03/IN-05 require the quality measurement output to include the cosine similarity distribution and false-positive rate, but `QualityMetrics` only contains `mean_score` and `false_positive_rate`. A mean is not a distribution, so the type catalogue cannot satisfy the stated measurement output contract.

AC-03/IN-05 require the quality command to report the cosine similarity distribution for threshold calibration, but `QualityMetrics` represents that distribution using `SimilarityScore`; `SimilarityScore` is defined as a clamped/normalized [0,1] value even though the same catalogue acknowledges raw cosine is [-1,1]. That means negative cosine values are collapsed before the mean/distribution is reported, so the plan no longer measures the cosine distribution it says it will measure.

`sotp find-similar` is specified to accept raw fragment text, and T008 constructs `FindSimilarCommand { fragment, top_k }` from that text, but the only fragment type is `CodeFragment` and it requires an originating `source_path`. The plan does not define a pathless query-fragment type or an explicit sentinel path, so the raw-text CLI contract is inconsistent with the type design and would require inventing fake source metadata.

AC-03/IN-05 require the quality command to output the cosine similarity distribution and false-positive rate, but `QualityMetrics` only carries mean, standard deviation, and a heuristic false-positive field. Since T009 only serializes these metrics, the plan cannot satisfy the stated measurement contract or provide the distribution needed for threshold calibration.

`false_positive_rate` is defined as the fraction of randomly sampled cross-file pairs above the threshold, treating them as non-duplicates. That is not a meaningful false-positive rate without labeled positives/negatives, so the plan's PoC metric does not actually measure the ADR/spec requirement it claims to cover.

AC-03/IN-05 require the quality command to report a false-positive rate, but the planned `QualityMetrics` only exposes `above_threshold_rate` and explicitly defines it as a proxy from unlabeled random cross-file pairs. Since T003/T009 only compute and serialize these metrics, the plan cannot satisfy the stated measurement contract unless it either adds labeled/evaluation input for a true false-positive rate or relaxes the spec to require the proxy metric instead.

The task-state-only briefing says there should be no catalogue/signal drift, but this generated domain type view now changes `SimilarFragment` to a fully signalled state, with `SemanticDupError` similarly changed on line 17. That is outside the requested T001 status bookkeeping change and should be reverted or moved to the implementation/signals scope.

The closing sentence overstates the license set: immediately above, `MPL-2.0` is explicitly classified as weak copyleft, and `RUSTSEC-2024-0436` is an advisory accept rather than a license. That makes the claim that all added items are "permissive or non-code-only" internally inconsistent.

The ADR says MPL-2.0 has no source-disclosure obligation when used unmodified, but MPL-2.0 source-availability obligations can still apply to MPL-covered files when distributing an executable/larger work even if those files were not modified. This is a semantic license-governance error in the consequence being recorded for the allowlist expansion.

This sentence says the CLI composition root in `T008/T009` extracts fragments using `T007`, but `T009`’s own task text says `dup-check` reads each file listed by `--files-from` directly into `CodeFragment` values. That makes the fragment-acquisition path inconsistent for `dup-check` and overstates `T007`’s role.

`T004` is now recorded as `done` with a commit hash, and the rendered plan marks it resolved, even though this plan-artifacts scope is described as only advancing `T002` and `T003` to `in_progress`. That makes the plan state claim completion of the infrastructure dependency task as part of unrelated T002/T003 bookkeeping, so the rendered task progress is semantically inconsistent with the scope/design intent.

The plan-artifact update is not limited to T005/T006 bookkeeping: the diff also changes T002 and T003 from `in_progress` to `done` and adds commit hashes, which contradicts the scope briefing's stated intent that only the T005/T006 status fields changed in `impl-plan.json`. This also drives the rendered `plan.md` resolved count and task checkboxes, so the artifacts no longer represent the described T005/T006-only update.

The `LanceDbSemanticIndexAdapter` row is marked 🔴 here, but the scope brief says the adapter structs are expected to remain 🟡 and that this view should have 0 red signals. That makes the rendered catalogue inconsistent with the intended bookkeeping state.

`LanceDbSemanticIndexAdapter` is marked `🔴` here, but this scope's intended state says the two adapter structs should remain `🟡` while only the four trait implementations flip to `🔵`. That makes the rendered catalogue inconsistent with the plan metadata for this round.

`search()` interprets LanceDB results as cosine similarity (`1.0 - _distance`, then clamp to `[0,1]`), but the query never sets `DistanceType::Cosine`. LanceDB `vector_search` defaults to L2, so this adapter will rank by L2 while reporting cosine-based scores, which can collapse most results to `0.0` and makes the returned similarities incorrect.

`search()` treats any `open_table()` failure as “table missing” and returns `Ok(Vec::new())`. That silently hides real storage errors such as permission failures, corruption, or schema problems; only `TableNotFound` should be converted to an empty result set.

`LanceDbSemanticIndexAdapter` calls `Runtime::block_on` in `new`, `insert`, and `search`. Tokio documents that `block_on` panics when invoked from an async execution context, so constructing or using this adapter from async code will crash instead of returning `SemanticIndexError`. That is a panic path in library code.

The first-insert path is a non-atomic `open_table(...); if missing -> create_table(...)` sequence. Because `insert` is `&self` and the adapter is `Send + Sync`, two callers can race on an empty database: both observe "table not found", one creates the table, and the other fails with a spurious `TableAlreadyExists`/`InsertFailed`, dropping the insert. Use LanceDB's create-if-exists mode or retry on table-exists.

`std::thread::spawn` panics if the OS cannot create a new thread, so this helper can crash the library instead of returning a `Result`. The same pattern also appears in `new()` at line 83, which means `insert`/`search` inherit an unrecoverable panic path under thread-exhaustion or spawn failure.

`to_string_lossy()` silently rewrites non-UTF-8 paths, so `new()` can open/create the wrong LanceDB directory on Unix, and `insert()` stores `source_path` values that cannot round-trip back to the original `PathBuf`. That loses user data for any fragment or database path containing invalid UTF-8 bytes.

A `current_thread` Tokio runtime is paired with `Handle::block_on` in both `new()` and `block_on_thread()`, but `Handle::block_on` does not drive a current-thread runtime. LanceDB's async `connect`/`open_table`/`add`/`search` futures will not make progress unless some thread is concurrently calling `Runtime::block_on`, so this adapter can hang or fail at startup and on every index operation.

`is_table_not_found` does not match LanceDB's actual missing-table error text (`Table '{name}' was not found`), so the fresh-database path never triggers. As a result, the intended first-run behavior in `insert` and `search` is broken: both will surface `InsertFailed`/`SearchFailed` instead of creating the table or returning an empty result set. Match `lancedb::Error::TableNotFound` directly instead of substring-searching `to_string()`.

`LanceDbSemanticIndexAdapter` owns a `tokio::runtime::Runtime` directly, but the adapter is explicitly made safe to construct and call from an existing Tokio executor by using bridge threads. If such an adapter is dropped inside that async context, Tokio's runtime destructor can panic while shutting down its blocking pool. This leaves a library-level panic path despite the no-panics rule; store the runtime in an `Option` and move/shutdown it from a non-runtime thread in `Drop`, or otherwise avoid owning a `Runtime` with normal in-context destruction.

`FastEmbedAdapter::new` does not honor the documented/plan-required `FASTEMBED_CACHE_PATH` cache override. The code uses `InitOptions::new(...)` without `with_cache_dir`, and fastembed 5.14's built-in lookup uses `FASTEMBED_CACHE_DIR`/`HF_HOME` instead, so CI or operators following this adapter's docs will have their pre-populated cache ignored and may trigger a runtime model download into the default cache location.

`dup_check` hard-codes `DUP_CHECK_TOP_K` to 10, so `SemanticIndexPort::search` can never return all above-threshold matches when more than 10 candidates qualify. That makes `DupCheckWarning.similar_fragments` incomplete and can suppress a warning that the soft gate should emit.

`measure_quality` evaluates every cross-file pair instead of using a random sample, but AC-03 defines the threshold-exceed metric as a random-sampled proxy. As written, the reported `above_threshold_rate` is biased and the routine scales as O(n^2) on larger workspaces.

`CodeFragment` documents and validates that `content` is always non-empty, but the `content` field is public, so callers can construct `CodeFragment { source_path, content: String::new() }` or clear it after `new()` succeeds, bypassing `SemanticDupError::EmptyContent`. This leaves the illegal empty-fragment state representable outside the constructor; keep the field private and expose read accessors if needed.
